text.title: eu-ai-act-compliance Failure Modes
text.summary: Five failure modes specific to LLM-driven AI Act compliance work.

section.title: Antipatterns

antipattern.description: Agent confidently cites nonexistent EU AI Act articles or invented Annex points.
antipattern.reason: LLMs interpolate plausible-looking article numbers from training data. A regulator who spots a fabricated citation will distrust the entire submission.

section.p: Symptom: rationale cites "Article 47" or other numbers that do not exist. Root cause: no citation verification step. Fix: add the reviewer subagent that cross-checks every citation against the official EUR-Lex text; pattern-validate citations syntactically in the validator script.

antipattern.description: System risk tier drifts as features are added, but the compliance pipeline is not re-triggered.
antipattern.reason: Adding biometric authentication to a "limited risk" recommender system flips it to high-risk; without a re-classification trigger the documentation lags reality and the system operates illegally.

section.p: Symptom: feature ships in production before its risk implications are reviewed. Root cause: classification treated as one-shot. Fix: integrate `scripts/validate-eu-ai-act-compliance.py` into CI as a gating check on any change touching the system description.

antipattern.description: Pipeline auto-submits generated compliance documents to regulators without explicit human legal review.
antipattern.reason: The Act treats submitted documentation as legally binding; un-reviewed agent output is a self-inflicted compliance breach.

section.p: Symptom: documents land at regulator with no reviewer attribution. Root cause: orchestrator skipped the hard stop. Fix: require `reviewer_signoff` in the schema; validator rejects any output without it; orchestrator must block on human sign-off.

antipattern.description: Running SHAP/LIME on a large transformer agent and shipping the resulting "explanations" as Article 13/14 satisfaction.
antipattern.reason: SHAP/LIME on transformers produces technically valid but practically uninterpretable surrogate explanations; the regulator wants meaningful interpretability, not numeric noise.

section.p: Symptom: regulator requests clarification on explanations; engineers cannot defend them. Root cause: blind application of explanation libraries. Fix: choose interpretability technique that matches the model class; document limitations explicitly; use behavioural eval over feature-attribution where appropriate.

antipattern.description: Compliance pipeline processes real user data for bias checks without honouring GDPR rights (DPIA, legal basis, retention).
antipattern.reason: GDPR applies independently of the AI Act; ignoring it during compliance work creates a second, distinct violation.

section.p: Symptom: data-subject request reveals AI Act bias-check dataset retains personal data beyond the agreed window. Root cause: parallel-regime confusion. Fix: stand up a DPIA for the compliance pipeline; pseudonymise or aggregate before bias check; set short retention.
